coredata.dc:title: In2S3/CuS nanosheet composite: An excellent visible light photocatalyst for H2 production from H2S
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Prakash, Arvind
Dan, Meng
Yu, Shan
Wei, Shiqian
Li, Yi
Wang, Fang
Zhou, Ying
coredata.dc:description: As hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a toxic chemical emitted in large quantities from both natural sources and industrial processes, resource utilization of H2S has become a hotspot research in recent years. We report herein, synthesis and characterization of a highly active In2S3/CuS nanosheet composite photocatalyst via a facile one-pot solvothermal route. The as-obtained In2S3/CuS composite show a special hierarchical structure and remarkable visible light absorption ability with an absorption edge of 785 nm. Its remarkable light absorption ability enables it to fully absorb the visible light. The as-obtained In2S3/CuS composite exhibits a superior visible-light photocatalytic activity and long-term durability in H2S splitting. As a result, a maximum H2 production rate of 14950 μmol g−1 h−1 over In2S3/CuS composite has been achieved even without any noble metal co-catalysts, which is 404 and 87 fold higher than that of over bare β-In2S3 and Cu9S5, respectively. The corresponding apparent quantum yield (AQY) of In2S3/CuS at 420 nm is 9.3%. The excellent photocatalytic activity depends on the chemical structure, phase, optical nature and folded nanosheet like geometry of In2S3/CuS composite, which facilitated electron transfer between interfaces of β-In2S3 and CuS, and improved the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, In2S3/CuS composite could serve as an efficient and practical visible-light response photocatalyst for H2 production from toxic and corrosive H2S.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Hydrogen
Hydrogen sulfide
In2S3/CuS composite
Visible light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the β-In2S3 and In2S3/CuS-80 composite.
TEM images, HRTEM images and SAED pattern of β-In2S3 (a, b, c), Cu9S5 (d, e, f) and In2S3/CuS-80 (g, h, i).
High resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p for Cu9S5 and In2S3/CuS (a), In 3d for β-In2S3 and In2S3/CuS (b), S 2p (c) and O 1s (d) of β-In2S3, Cu9S5 and In2S3/CuS.
The UV–Vis diffused reflectance spectra β-In2S3, Cu9S5 and In2S3/Cu2S-80 composites.
(a) and (b) Photocatalytic H2 production rate of theses samples under visible light irradiation; (c) cycling experiments; (d) H2 production rate in different reaction medium.
a) In 3d; b) Cd2p; c) S2p XPS spectra of the In2S3/CuS-80 sample after long-term catalytic reaction.
Transient photocurrent analysis of β-In2S3, Cu9S5 and In2S3/CuS-80 composite under visible-light irradiation.
Photocatalytic process of splitting H2S over In2S3/CuS composites under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: In2S3/CuS nanosheet composite: An excellent visible light photocatalyst for H2 production from H2S

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a toxic chemical emitted in large quantities from both natural sources and industrial processes, resource utilization of H2S has become a hotspot research in recent years. We report herein, synthesis and characterization of a highly active In2S3/CuS nanosheet composite photocatalyst via a facile one-pot solvothermal route. The as-obtained In2S3/CuS composite show a special hierarchical structure and remarkable visible light absorption ability with an absorption edge of 785 nm. Its remarkable light absorption ability enables it to fully absorb the visible light. The as-obtained In2S3/CuS composite exhibits a superior visible-light photocatalytic activity and long-term durability in H2S splitting. As a result, a maximum H2 production rate of 14950 μmol g−1 h−1 over In2S3/CuS composite has been achieved even without any noble metal co-catalysts, which is 404 and 87 fold higher than that of over bare β-In2S3 and Cu9S5, respectively. The corresponding apparent quantum yield (AQY) of In2S3/CuS at 420 nm is 9.3%. The excellent photocatalytic activity depends on the chemical structure, phase, optical nature and folded nanosheet like geometry of In2S3/CuS composite, which facilitated electron transfer between interfaces of β-In2S3 and CuS, and improved the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, In2S3/CuS composite could serve as an efficient and practical visible-light response photocatalyst for H2 production from toxic and corrosive H2S.

ce:section.ce:para: Progress in clean energy technology requires terrific uprising in renewable energy research attributable to the global energy and environmental disasters. Innovations with new approaches are producing fuels alternatives. Hydrogen (H2) is becoming one of the leading competitors for future fuel, which has the highest energy density (120–142 MJ/kg) in comparison to any other fuel without carbon trace [1-10]. Concerning the economic and environmental advantages, hydrogen sulfide (H2S) has drawn more attention due to possess less decomposition energy (∆G° = 33.3 kJ/mol), to convert into hydrogen and sulfur [11]. H2S is a regular noxious waste generated during petroleum refining, gasification of coal and very little quantity in biochemical process [12]. An abundant availability in various natural and industrial resources reveals that it remains an ultimate foundation for the H2 production with a great economic value. However, the inexpensive and conventional production of H2 from toxic and corrosive H2S is still a significant scientific confront. The traditional Claus process has been widely utilized in industry to realize the removal of H2S via partial oxidation of H2S into sulfur and water where H2 is wasted, thus this process has some economic and environmental drawbacks [12]. In 1975, Raymont recognized dry box process followed by oxide revivification for decomposition of H2S into hydrogen and elemental sulfur, although the equilibrium constant of thermolysis reaction did not favor to proceed [13]. On the other hand, after the successful research on photoelectrocatalytic water splitting by Fujishima and Honda in 1972, the hydrogen production by splitting H2O or H2S over various types of semiconductor photocatalysts such as metal oxide and metal sulfide has been studied [14–23,30–34]. A first successful application for decomposition of H2S into hydrogen and sulfur was reported by Nick Serpone in 1984 via electron transfer from the conduction band of CdS to that of TiO2 particles occurs in alkaline suspensions containing SH- ions under visible light irradiation [36]. Subsequently, hydrogen production via photocatalytic H2S splitting has become a clean, economical, and environmental friendly approach. In the past few decades, several active metal sulfide photocatalysts have been developed for H2S splitting under solar energy irradiation, which elucidate better charge transfer processes and improve the efficiency to disintegrate H2S [19–23,31–34,36]. Nevertheless an obvious regular improvement in activity and stability of photocatalyst along with better efficiency of hydrogen production via H2S disintegrations is still a challenge for its practical applications. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop a highly efficient catalyst with elevated activity and stability under visible light illumination.

For this purpose, metal sulfide systems have attracted great attentions due to their unique properties like appropriate band gap, crystalline structure and catalytic functions [16–18,23,31–34]. Among these metal sulfides, In2S3 with a narrow band gap of 2.0–2.2 eV existing in three different crystalline forms like defect cubic, defect spinel and layered structures [37]. However, quick recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs resulted in low efficient, which is still a confrontation to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of In2S3. Consequently, various researches have been emerged for the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency of In2S3 via different strategies, such as the modification of In2S3 morphologies, integrating with doping of metal and carbon materials, use of cocatalyst and composite formation between approximate similar band structure semiconductors [23,38–42]. Among them, the construction of composite is a promising strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of bulk In2S3. Over the past decade, various In2S3-based composites such as MnS/In2S3 [23], In2S3/ZnS [24,25], In2S3/CdS [26,27], ZnO/In2S3 [28] and In2S3/g-C3N4 [29] have been explored as appropriate photocatalyst with a relatively high performance for respective photocatalytic reaction. Meanwhile, copper sulfides (CuS), as one of the narrow band gap semiconductor, have aroused significant interests in the photocatalysis due to their variable stoichiometric composition, valence states, crystal morphologies and different unique properties [43,44,48–54]. Therefore, In2S3 accompanied to copper sulfide, could be a promising active photocatalyst through utilizing band gap engineering and matching crystal lattices for photocatalytic H2 production from H2S. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the photocatalytic H2 production from H2S over In2S3/CuS composite.

Herein, the In2S3/CuS nanosheet composite was successfully fabricated by one-pot solvothermal method. The composite exhibits superior visible light absorption ability. The photocatalytic H2 evolution has been studied under visible light irradiation over In2S3/CuS composite from H2S decomposition using S2-/SO3 2- as reaction medium, corresponding to H2 evolution rate of 14590 μmol g−1 h−1, which is approximately 404 times higher than of pristine β-In2S3 (37 μmol g−1 h−1) and 87 times higher than that of over bare Cu9S5 (171 μmol g−1 h−1). The corresponding apparent quantum yield (AQY) of In2S3/CuS at 420 nm is 9.3%. The superior visible light photocatalytic activity can be ascribed to the excellent electron-hole separation activity and the synergy effects of the β-In2S3 and CuS. Simultaneously, the introduction of CuS within composite material generates appropriate chemical structure, band gap energy in favor of facile transportation of photo-carriers, which is also favorable to enhance the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the role of reaction medium (S2-/SO3 2-) was also discussed. Finally, the photocatalytic H2 production process over In2S3/CuS composite is discussed.

For the synthesis of composite, two solutions were prepared; InCl3·2H2O (100-x mmol) was dissolved in 1 pH of aqueous HCl. An appropriate amount of Na2S solution was gradually added to the solution, with continuous stirring, to obtain a homogeneous yellow sol (the mol ratio of In/S = 1/2) as solution A. And copper acetate (x mmol) was dissolved in ethylenediamine with suitable amount of thioacetamide (TAA) by using a magnetic stirrer obtains another dark brown homogeneous sol as solution B. For expediency, the In2S3/CuS composites were designated as In2S3/CuS-80, In2S3/CuS-60, In2S3/CuS-40 and In2S3/CuS-20 corresponding to the (100-x) value of 80, 60, 40 and 20 respectively. The solution A was dropwise added into the solution B using a magnetic stirrer followed by adding diluted HCl to fix the pH of solution at 3 and immediately transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with 50 mL inner volume. And then the autoclave was maintained at 180 ℃ for 24 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, the obtained orange red precipitate was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and water several times. Finally, In2S3/CuS composites powder was obtained followed by overnight drying at 60 ℃. The β-In2S3 and Cu9S5 were also prepared by a similar above-mentioned method using respective solution.

The structure and crystallinity of the samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (PANalyticalX’pert) with Cu Kαradiation operated at 40 kV/40 mA. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) was recorded using a JEOLJSM-7800F microscopy equipped with EDAX detector. Tecnai G2 F30 electron microscopy was used with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV for observing Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and all of the binding energies were referenced to the C1s level at 284.6 eV. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were recorded at room temperature on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as the reflectance standard. Photocurrent and electrochemical measurement was done with CHI 660D workstation by using Pt as a counter, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference and β-In2S3, Cu9S5 and In2S3/CuS composite modified fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates using doctor blade technique (film thickness: ca. 50 µm) as working electrodes in the 0.6 M Na2SO3 as an electrolyte under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm).

A home-made photoreactor (50 mL Pyrex flask) was used for the measurements of photocatalytic activity [23]. 2.5 mg of photocatalyst powder was suspended in an aqueous solution (50 mL) containing Na2S (0.1 mol L−1) and Na2SO3 (0.6 mol L−1) followed by 30 min ultrasonication. After that, the reactor was evacuated with purging Ar for 30 min followed by flowing 3 M H2S for 3 h at room temperature. After completing the above procedure, the reactor was illuminated by a 300-W Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm). The amount of produced hydrogen was monitored at every one hour upto six hours study under photoctalysis using a Shimadzu GC-2010 Plus gas chromatograph (Ar carrier gas, molecular sieve 5 Å,TCD detector).

The apparent quantum yield (AQY) was calculated according to Eq. (1). The number of evolved H2 molecules was measured by GC (Shimadzu GC-2010 Plus) and the number of incident photons at 450 nm was determined from the output of a monochromatic LED lamp (89 mW/cm2).
(1)
QE
( % ) =
number
of
reacted
electrons
number
of
incident
photos
× 100
=
2 ×
number
of
evolved
H
2

× 100

The crystal structure of the as-synthesized samples was analyzed by powder XRD as shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. In the absence of Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O, the obtained sample can be indexed into β-In2S3 (JCPDS 32-0456). The diffraction peaks at 27.58, 33.28, 43.79 and 47.93 corresponding to the (311), (400), (511) and (440) planes of β-In2S3. With the introduction of Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O, the additional diffraction peaks can be observed, except for β-In2S3, which can be indexed to the (004), (100), (101), and (103) planes of hexagonal CuS phase (JCPDS 65-3929). This result indicates that the In2S3/CuS-80 composite was successfully obtained by this facile method. Additionally, it is worthwhile mentioning that the Cu9S5 (JCPDS 26-0476) can be prepared in absence of InCl3, as shown in Fig. S1. The result illustrates that the copper sulfide can be transformed into copper sulfides (CuxS) with different stoichiometric composition in different condition, which may be favorable for the formation of In2S3/CuS composite with an intimate contact. It is commonly known that the intimate contact share important for increasing photocatalytic activity by reducing the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes [45–47].

The morphology and microstructure of β-In2S3, Cu9S5 and In2S3/CuS-80 were explored by TEM, high resolution TEM (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) as shown in Fig. 2. TEM image of β-In2S3 displays a 2D nanoflakes-like morphology (Fig. 2a). Moreover, the aggregated structure can be also clearly observed, it is demonstrated that the single β-In2S3 is easily aggregated. As shown in Fig. 2b, the HRTEM image illustrated the lattice spacing 0.32, 0.34 and 0.52 nm matches well with the (311), (400), and (440) crystal planes of β-In2S3, which is in good accord with SAED (Fig. 2c) and XRD pattern [29]. The pure Cu9S5 also exhibit a 2D nanoflakes-like morphology (Fig. 2d). The similar morphology may be favorable for the formation of In2S3/CuS composite. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2e demonstrated that the lattice spacing is about 0.19, and 0.32 nm, which can be assigned to the (110), and (0015) plane of Cu9S5, respectively (Fig. 2f) [54]. The In2S3/CuS-80 composite was showed a 2D folded thin nanosheet morphology (Fig. 2g), which is further analyze by HRTEM (Fig. 2h) indicates that the In2S3/CuS composite, which are in good agreement with XRD pattern (Fig. 2i) [39,54]. The above results confirmed that the In2S3/CuS-80 nanosheet composite consists bonding interaction between the interface of matching crystal lattices of β-In2S3 and CuS which facilitate direct electron transfer through interfacial charge transfer for enhancement of H2 production rate [49,55-57]. This nanosheet like geometry of In2S3/CuS-80 composite was developed due to the large variation in solubility products (Ksp) of β-In2S3 (5.7 × 10–73) and CuS (6.3 × 10–36) via preferential transformation of in situ nucleate CuS nanocrystal on the surface of In2S3 due to lowest activation energy of nucleation at interface [49,58]. Additionally ethylenediamine could play an important role as structure directing template for generating nanosheet like structure of In2S3/CuS-80 composite [59].

The elemental distribution and content of In2S3/CuS-80 composite is ascertained by EDXS, as shown in Fig. S2. The EDXS spectrum clearly showed that the In2S3/CuS composite was composed of indium, copper and sulfur elements. The weight and atomic percentages of species detected by EDXS are shown in Table S1. The atomic percentage of indium (0.05%), cooper (0.05%) and sulfur (0.13%) suggested the presence of β-In2S3 and CuS with in In2S3/CuS-80 composite and the presence of high percentage of Si due to the use of silicon wafer for sample preparation. This observation sustains the existence of In2S3/CuS composite.

The XPS was used to further investigate the surface chemical composition of the β-In2S3, Cu9S5, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite. The XPS survey of In2S3/CuS-80 composite indicates the presence of In, Cu and S (Fig. S3), which is in good agreement with the EDXS. The presence of C peak can be attributed to adventitious carbon species from XPS tests. Fig. 3a shows the Cu 2p of Cu9S5, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite, respectively. The In2S3/CuS-80 composite had two strong peaks located at 931.5 and 951.4 eV assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 correspond to CuS with a shift of 0.3 eV [52–54]. The result illustrated the presence of strong interaction between β-In2S3 and CuS. Additionally, the pure cooper sulfide samples correspond to Cu9S5, had two additional weak peaks at 933.9 and 954.2 eV are indexed to CuO. Moreover, two satellite peaks at 944.4 and 962.8 eV were also observed, which correspond to Cu2+ [40,45]. The results indicate that the formation of In2S3/CuS-80 composite can prevent the partial superficially oxidation [52,54,61]. The In 3d peaks can be assign to In3+ in β-In2S3 are identified at 452.1 and 444.5 eV ascribed to In 3d3/2 and In 3d1/2 for pure β-In2S3, while with a small shift of 0.1 eV at 452.0 and 444.4 eV for In2S3/CuS-80 composite (Fig. 3b) [59]. Fig. 3c exhibits the S 2p peaks of Cu9S5, β-In2S3, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite, respectively. The binding energy of S 2p in β-In2S3, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite are not changed, which can be assigned to S2-. Additionally, the peak at 167.8 eV corresponds to the SO4 2− in β-In2S3/CuS-80 composite. Compared to In2S3/CuS-80 composite, this peak in Cu9S5 shifted to 168.3 eV, which illustrates that Cu9S5 was more easily oxidized than CuS [49,54,60,62]. Fig. 3d shows that the O 1s peaks of Cu9S5, β-In2S3, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite. The amount of O element can be found on the surface of all the samples, which could be attributed to the surface absorbed hydroxyl and water. All of these results indicated the presence of strong interactions between CuS and β-In2S3 within composite.

The optical properties of all the samples were studied by the UV–vis DRS, as shown in Fig. 4. The In2S3/CuS-80 composite shows broad absorption ability hardly occupy whole visible region, which may be favorable to realize high visible-light photocatalysis in comparison to that of β-In2S3 and Cu9S5. It must also be mentioned that the UV–vis DRS profile of the In2S3/CuS composite present a combination of the spectral feature of β-In2S3 and CuS alone, corresponding to absorption edge of 670.3 nm and 784.8 nm, respectively. This result again confirms that In2S3/CuS composite was successfully prepared with superior visible-light absorption ability. Here, the superior visible-light absorption ability of In2S3/CuS-80 composite can be attributed to the introduction of CuS. However, the absorption spectra of pure copper sulfide shows absorption maximum at 300 nm confirm the presence of Cu9S5 [54]. The band gap energy value of β-In2S3 and Cu9S5 were calculated by the onset of the absorption edge, the band gap energies corresponding to E g = 2.12 eV and 1.96 eV for as synthesized β-In2S3 and Cu9S5 respectively. All in all, the superior visible-light absorption ability is promising for realizing the excellent photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic performances of all the samples were evaluated in the solution of Na2S/Na2SO3 along with 3 M H2S concentration for 6 h under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), and the results are summarized in Fig. 5a and b. Firstly, the blank experiment revealed that H2 was not detected without photocatalyst. The pure β-In2S3 and Cu9S5 show a relatively poor photocatalytic activity for H2 production (37 μmol g−1 h−1 and 171 μmol g−1 h-1, respectively, cf. Fig. 5b), which could be attributed to the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The In2S3/CuS-80 composite exhibited an admirable photocatalytic H2 production from H2S activity (14950 μmol g−1 h−1) even without loading any co-catalyst, which is more than 404 times higher than β-In2S3 and 87 times higher than Cu9S5, respectively. It is experienced that a momentous sum of bubbles were created during the photocatalytic reaction, confirming the high activity of In2S3/CuS-80 composite. The result clearly indicates the formed heterojunction structure between β-In2S3 and CuS can facilitate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers.

The photostability of a photocatalysts is crucial for practical applications. The recycling performance was evaluated over In2S3/CuS-80 composite within three cycles along with a 3 h study in each cycle. The stability of photocatalyst was examined to find out the effectiveness by reusing the photocatalyst to its maximum extent. It was observed that the effectiveness of photocatalyst was slightly decreased after its third usage as shown in Fig. 5c. Though, this result of H2 production by H2S decomposition over reused photocatalyst showed the good photostability and anti-photocorrosive character of the In2S3/CuS-80 composite. Generally, the activity of H2 production from H2S was reported up to first 3–4 h, but long term analysis was rarely identified. Furthermore, the long-term stability of In2S3/CuS-80 composite photocatalysts was also investigated and found an increasing H2 evolution rate up to 13 h (Fig. S4). This phenomenon illustrates that the In2S3/CuS-80 composite exhibits excellent photocatalytic stability and anti-photocorrosion capability during the photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). In addition, further studies were performed to investigate the stability of the In2S3/CuS-80 composite. First, the surface chemical composition of the In2S3/CuS-80 sample after long-term catalytic reaction was investigated by the XPS analysis, as shown in Fig. 6. The comparison of the high-resolution spectra of In 3d and Cu 2p of In2S3/CuS-80 sample before and after the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction, we found that it did not change obviously. However, It is worth noting that the S 2p XPS spectra of In2S3/CuS-80 composite has an significant change, and there is one additional peak. This peak located at 168.0 eV can be assigned to sodium thiosulfate (S2O3 2-). This result demonstrates the formation of S2O3 2- during the reaction process can improve the stability of catalyst [23]. Meanwhile, the TEM and HRTEM images of this sample were shown in Fig. S5. The TEM images of In2S3/CuS-80 did not show any change after irradiation for 13 h, confirming that the structural is stable. The HRTEM and SAED analysis resultS further demonstrate the existence of β-In2S3 and CuS. These results demonstrate that the In2S3/CuS photocatalysts are essentially stable. A comparison on the performance of the reported materials (Table S2) justifies the advantages of the present nanocomposite in H2 production from photocatalytic decomposition of H2S.

In addition, the role of reaction medium was recognized on comparing the rate of H2 evolution in the presence of S2- and SO3 2- (14950 μmol g−1 h−1), SO3 2- and OH- (490 μmol g−1 h−1 and) and OH- alone (86 μmol g−1 h−1) revealed that Na2SO3 and Na2S play an important role as the sacrificial reagents to consume photogenerated holes and delayed the recombination to improve the anti-photocorrosion ability of catalysts and H2 production efficiency (Fig. 5d) [23]. Different medium like KHC8H4O4, HCl and H2SO4 were used within controlled pH in the range of 7.5–8.0 by replacing H2S for confirming the produced H2 source is merely H2S or H2O. The data of different alternative of H2S are summarized in Table S3, that confirm that the major portion of H2 production contributed from H2S in 0.1 M Na2S/0.6 M Na2SO3/3 M H2S reaction medium.

All in all, the In2S3/CuS-80 composite super high photocatalytic performance can be following reasons: (1) Superior visible-light absorption ability, which improved the utilization of solar energy. (2) The folded nanosheet morphology of composite material, which is favorable to promoting the diffusion of reactants and products and enhancing light harvesting [35]. (3) Na2S/Na2SO3 as reaction medium may prolong the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pair at the surface of photocatalyst and in that way contributes in the efficient H2 production.

It is well accepted that the high photocatalytic activity is due to the effective separation of photoexcited electrons-holes pairs. To explore the effective separation of photoexcited electrons-holes pairs, the photocatalytic efficiency of all the above samples used in the H2 production were implemented by the transient photocurrent response of all the samples was measured, as show in Fig. 7. All the samples show relatively stable and reversible photoresponses at light-on and light-off under visible-light irradiation. The In2S3/CuS-80 exposed the highest photocurrent approximately 3.0 μA cm−2 in compare to that of β-In2S3 and Cu9S5 ( 1.0 and 0.1 μA cm−2), indicating the efficient separation of photo-generated electrons holes along with reproduce constant photocurrent under visible light irradiation illustrate delayed recombination process. In addition to, the electro-chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plot was carried out to explore the ability of the interfacial charge transfer (Fig. S6). The diameter of the arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot of In2S3/CuS-80 composite is smaller than that for β-In2S3 and Cu9S5, suggesting that In2S3/CuS-80 composite can facilitate the interfacial charge transfer. Moreover, Fig. 6 (inset) shows the equivalent circuit model fitted by the obtained data and the values of the fitting circuit are summarized in Table S4. Notably, the resistances of the working electrode (R2) are 5300, 1321 and 1400 Ω for the β-In2S3, In2S3/CuS and CuS, respectively. This result again demonstrates that the formation of In2S3/CuS heterojuction can promote the interfacial charge transfer. Fig. S7 shows the photoluminescence spectrum of these samples. It was observed that the emission intensity of In2S3/CuS-80 was significantly weakened, implying that the combination of charge carriers was effectively decreased.

Moreover, a sufficiently negative conduction band (CB) potential is essential for the efficient photocatalytic H2 production. Here, the XPS valence band (VB) spectrum of β-In2S3 and In2S3/CuS-80 composite were implemented, as shown in Fig. S8. The VB maximum is located at 1.12 eV and 0.35 eV, whereas the Eg of β-In2S3, and In2S3/CuS-80 composite is 1.85 eV, and 1.58 eV respectively analyzed from UV–vis spectrum. Therefore, their minimum of conduction band should be occurs at − 0.73 eV, and − 1.23 eV respectively. Compared with the pure β-In2S3, the band structure of the In2S3/CuS-80 composite with a relatively appropriate redox potential, which is favorable to photocatalytic H2 production from H2S.

The visible light photocatalytic H2-production mechanism from H2S decomposition over In2S3/CuS-80 folded nanosheets is proposed as illustrated in Fig. 8. The band gap positions of the conduction and valence band of CuS and β-In2S3 were calculated by using the empirical equations [63,64]:
E
C B
= X −
E
e
− 0.5
E
g
,
where E
CB is the conduction band energy, E
g is the band energy of the semiconductor (estimated from the onset of the absorption edges shown in Fig. 4.), E
e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (~ 4.5 eV for normal hydrogen electrode), and X is the electronegativity of the material, which can be expressed as the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms. Here, the calculated values of X are 4.70 eV and 5.28 eV for β-In2S3 and CuS, respectively. E
VB (valence band edge potential) is determined by the equation:
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E
g
.
According to the above equation, the conduction band edges of β-In2S3 and CuS are −0.73 eV and −0.01 ev, respectively. Accordingly, the valence band edges are 1.12 eV for β-In2S3 and 1.57 eV for CuS.

As a result, the β-In2S3 and CuS samples were irradiated by a photon with sufficient energy equal or larger than Eg, both β-In2S3 and CuS are excited, and the photogenerated electrons and holes are in their conduction and valence bands, respectively. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons of β-In2S3 can transfer to CuS due to the suitable energy band, where the reduction of H+ occurs; and the photoexcited holes in CuS transfer from its valence band to that of β-In2S3, where the holes in the VB of β-In2S3 could be consumed by the oxidation reaction as shown in Fig. 8. All in all, the formation of In2S3/CuS heterojuction can effectively enhance the separation efficiency of the photoexcited electrons and holes, which is crucial to improve the photocatalytic activity.

In addition to, the major reaction steps in this mechanism under visible light irradiation are described in following equations.
(2) H2S + OH- ↔ HS- + H2O
(3) Photocatalyst + hν → h+ VB+e- CB
(4) 2HS- + 2h+ VB → 2H++S°
(5) 2H+ + 2e- CB → H2↑
(6) S0 + SO3 2- → S2O3 2-
(7) SO3 2- + S2- + 2h+ VB → S2O3 2-

When In2S3/CuS composite were irradiated by a photon with sufficient energy equal or larger than Eg, they will be excited, and the photogenerated electrons and holes are produced (Eq. (3)). The H2 is generated via collection of electrons by H+ at CuS (Eq. (5)), and H+ is produced by decomposition of HS- after accepting hole at VB of β-In2S3 (Eqs. (2) and (4)), this process designate here that H2S is the central source for hydrogen production. In this photocatalysis reaction HS-and SO3 2- species are photo-oxidized into S and S2O3 2− ions by capturing photogenerated holes over In2S3/CuS-80 (Eqs. (4), (6) and (7)). The production of yellow S2 2− and solid S were prevented by sufficient amount of SO3 2- in 0.6 M Na2SO3/0.1 M Na2S, which leads to an impressively high H2 production rate and improves the long-term stability [23,65].

The In2S3/CuS-80 nanosheet composite was successfully fabricated by one-pot solvothermal method. The In2S3/CuS-80 composite show a high visible-light photocatalytic H2 production activity (14950 μmol g−1 h−1) from H2S even in the absence of noble-metal co-catalyst, the corresponding apparent quantum yield (AQY) at 420 nm is 9.3%. This result indicates the composite formation is an efficient strategy to improve the photocatalytic activity. Simultaneously, the superior visible-light absorption ability of In2S3/CuS composite is an important reason for the excellent photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the 0.1 M Na2S/0.6 M Na2SO3/3 M H2S are optimized reaction media, which could not only improve H2 evaluation but also promote the formation of colorless S2O3 2 − and favor long-term H2 production. We suppose that this work may provide key information and opinion for new plans based on metal sulfide photocatalyst, which may generate a platform for industrial applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank financial supports by the Sichuan Provincial International Cooperation Project (2017HH0030), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21403172), Sichuan Youth Science and Technology Foundation (2014JQ0017) and the Innovative Research Team of Sichuan Province (2016TD0011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.solmat.2018.03.011.
